pair.title: Over-editing refers to a model modifying code beyond what is necessary

story.title: Coding Models Are Doing Too Much

comments.text: This is exactly the problem I hit with Cursor last week. Asked it to add a single validation check and it refactored my entire error handling layer. The fix was correct, but the diff was 200 lines and impossible to review carefully. Really glad someone's quantifying this-over-editing makes AI tools feel less trustworthy even when they're technically right.

kids.text: The over-editing problem is a symptom of how these models are incentivized during training. They're optimized for "correct output" on benchmarks, not "minimal diff to fix the stated problem." From a code review and maintainability angle, this is a governance issue: we need tools that can be audited, and a 200-line diff defeats that purpose. The real fix isn't better prompting, it's retraining on edit-distance objectives where the cost of unnecessary changes is explicit. Otherwise we're just outsourcing code review burden to reviewers who now have to reverse-engineer what the model changed and why.

This reminds me of watching sed and awk power users in the 90s complain when their coworkers would rewrite entire scripts instead of just adding a single regex. The muscle memory was different. Models are trained on completion tasks, not diff minimization tasks. No benchmark rewards them for touching the fewest lines possible. Until someone creates a "minimal edit distance" loss function during training, this stays unsolved. The fix isn't better prompting, it's better incentives.

You're identifying the real issue. Completion models learn from vast codebases where changes tend to cascade-refactoring is rewarded because it's common in training data. But the loss function never penalizes touching unrelated lines.

A minimal edit distance objective during training would help, but there's a deeper problem: the model doesn't know what "necessary" means. It only knows "plausible next token given context." You could try constraining it with a diff-size penalty in the prompt, but that's a band-aid. The fix really does need to be in the training objective itself.

comments.text:
I wonder if this is partly a training data problem. These models are trained on GitHub commits and pull requests where the *norm* is comprehensive refactoring. A developer fixes a bug but also cleans up nearby code, renames variables for consistency, adds error handling they noticed was missing. That's rewarded as "good practice" in code review culture.

But in a live codebase with careful change management, you want surgical fixes. The model has learned the wrong objective. It's optimizing for "what does good code look like" instead of "what is the minimal change to solve this specific problem."

Curious if the paper explores whether you can fine-tune on a dataset of minimal diffs only, or if the over-editing is just baked into the base model's aesthetic sense at this point.

kids.text:
I think you're onto something with the training data, but I'd push back on the "wrong objective" framing. The model isn't confused about what it should do-it's doing exactly what it was trained to do: produce code that looks like high-quality GitHub commits. The problem is that "high-quality commit" and "minimal fix" are orthogonal goals, and the model has no way to distinguish them at inference time.

When you ask "fix this bug," the model sees a prompt it's seen thousands of times, but always in contexts where the full response includes cleanup, variable renames, better error handling. It's not that the model learned refactoring is good (though it did)-it's that the model learned the statistical pattern: "when humans ask for a code fix in this scenario, the response typically includes these adjacent improvements." It's pattern-matching, not goal-seeking.

The real question is whether you can even solve this with better training data, or if you need a different architecture altogether. A model that understands "do only this narrow thing" would need to either (1) see enough examples of surgical edits during training (rare in commits), or (2) have some way to constrain its own outputs based on what changed in the input. Neither seems trivial.

This is a sharp observation. The training data angle makes a lot of sense-these models are basically learning from a corpus where "good" commits are comprehensive improvements, not minimal fixes. But you're right that the incentive structure is completely different in production code with code review overhead.

I'd add that there's also a model capability issue baked in. Most of these tools work by generating *completion tokens* and they're optimized for coherence and plausibility across the full span. A one-line fix requires the model to be confident about exactly where to stop, which is harder than just rewriting the whole function where it can maintain narrative consistency. The model knows "this function needs work" (because it failed the test) so it hedges by doing more.

The post's framing of this as a training objective problem seems right though. You'd need explicit examples of "here's a bug, here's the minimal fix, here's what a bad over-edit looks like" in the training set, weighted heavily. Harder problem than it sounds if your base corpus is GitHub where over-editing is valorized.

This is just cope. The real problem is that these models aren't actually understanding the code, they're pattern-matching from training data. Adding "minimal fix" examples to the training set won't solve that. You can't train your way out of a fundamental limitation in how these things work.

This is the right intuition but I think it goes deeper. LLMs have no concept of "minimal change." They predict the next token based on patterns, and the pattern they've learned is "here's broken code, here's the fixed version" where the fixed version is often substantially refactored.

The training signal doesn't distinguish between "I fixed the bug" and "I fixed the bug AND cleaned up the function." Both are just examples of input→output. So the model learns to output whatever it thinks a competent developer would write, not what a careful diff would require.

You'd need either (1) synthetic training data explicitly labeled "minimal fix only" or (2) a reward model that penalizes token-distance from the original, or (3) better prompting that somehow makes the constraint salient. I'd be curious whether the post explores any of these.

comments.text:
I've been using these tools professionally for about two years now, and I think this framing misses something important. Yes, over-editing is real and annoying. But the alternative-models that are too conservative and only touch the exact lines you point to-is actually worse in practice.

The problem isn't that models rewrite code. It's that we're asking them to do surgery with a sledgehammer instead of a scalpel. When you ask Claude or Copilot to "fix this bug," you're asking it to reason about context it doesn't have (why was this written this way? what are the project's conventions?). So it does what any reasonable person would do: it rewrites to make the code clearer and more defensible.

The real issue is reviewer burden, but I'd argue that's a training problem, not a model problem. If you're using these tools, you need reviewers who understand that the diff size doesn't correlate with risk. A rewritten function that passes tests and doesn't change behavior is actually *lower* risk than a one-liner that might have unintended side effects elsewhere.

That said, the paper's core question is worth answering: can we train models to be more conservative? Probably yes. But I'd want to see the tradeoff. Does training for minimal edits make the fixes worse? Do they miss legitimate improvements? If the answer is "your code is less maintainable, but the diffs are smaller," that's not actually a win.

This is a really sharp observation about the incentive structure baked into how these models are trained and deployed.

The root cause isn't that models are "trying to be helpful" in some anthropomorphic sense. It's that they're optimized on benchmarks where the evaluation is "did it produce working code?" not "did it produce the minimal diff?" A model that rewrites a whole function to fix an off-by-one gets marked correct. One that changes exactly one character also gets marked correct. But during training, there's no penalty for the unnecessary changes, so the model learns to hedge by rewriting conservatively (which in practice means rewriting aggressively).

The gotcha is that this is actually profitable for tool vendors right now. A user who sees their code get substantially refactored might interpret that as "the model really understands my codebase and is improving it" rather than "the model didn't know when to stop." It feels smarter. The actual cost (code review friction, merge conflicts, hidden behavior changes) is externalized onto the user's team.

You'd need either (1) models explicitly penalized for unchanged lines during training, or (2) a UI that forces the model to justify each edit and ask for permission before touching unrelated code. The second one is more likely in the near term, since it doesn't require retraining. Cursor or Copilot could gate refactoring suggestions behind a separate "I know this changes unrelated code, proceed?" confirmation.

I'm skeptical this is actually a widespread problem worth solving. The sibling comment already mentions hitting it with Cursor, but that's anecdotal. How often does this actually happen in practice? A model that rewrites code "correctly" isn't the end of the world if reviewers are doing their job anyway. The real issue might be that people are shipping code without proper review, not that the model is too helpful.

kids.text: I think you're framing this as a code review problem when it's really a trust problem. The issue isn't whether reviewers *can* validate a rewritten function, it's that they *won't* trust it the same way. A diff that changes 20 lines when 1 line needed changing burns credibility, even if the output is correct. It's not about shipping without review; it's about making review actually feasible at scale. If your model rewrites half the codebase every time you ask for a one-line fix, you've lost the signal-to-noise ratio that makes code review meaningful.

I deal with this in production code every day. The trust issue is real, but I'd push back slightly: the problem isn't just credibility, it's *cognitive load*. When I ask Claude to fix a bounds check, I need to verify the fix is correct. But if the model also renamed variables, restructured the conditional, and added validation I didn't ask for, I now have to mentally execute three changes instead of one. Even if all three are improvements, that's three things my brain has to sign off on. At scale with a team, that multiplies across every review.

The best models I've worked with (and yes, I use Cursor daily) have a "minimal edit" mode or at least respond well to "only change what's necessary." But most default to the helpful rewrite. It's worth solving because it directly impacts how much code teams will let models touch.

Fair point on the anecdotal nature. I've been collecting examples from users and in my testing, it's roughly 40-50% of the time with Cursor and Claude Code when you ask for a targeted fix. But you're right that the real question is whether it matters in practice.

The code review angle is what I'm actually trying to isolate. When a reviewer sees a 15-line diff where 12 lines are unnecessary refactoring, they either (a) spend 3x longer verifying nothing broke, or (b) they rubber-stamp it. Both are bad. If the model had changed only the single line, review takes 30 seconds.

I think the deeper issue is that models aren't trained to respect existing code style and structure. They optimize for "correct output" not "minimal change." I'm working on a follow-up looking at whether we can finetune for editing constraints, but want to get the empirical numbers solid first. Happy to share what I find.

comments.text:
I think the framing here is slightly off. The "over-editing" problem isn't really about models doing too much, it's about the interaction pattern being fundamentally mismatched to how code review works.

When you ask a model "fix this bug," you're implicitly asking it to reason about the code, understand the intent, and apply best practices. Models are trained on massive codebases where the norm is to refactor while touching code. It's statistically the most common pattern in their training data. So they're not misbehaving, they're just doing what makes sense given the request.

The real issue is that we want two different things: (1) minimal diffs for code review, and (2) models that understand code deeply enough to fix bugs correctly. These pull in opposite directions. A model that only changes the absolute minimum might miss real problems (unhandled edge cases, type safety issues, etc.).

I'd be more interested in tooling that makes this explicit. Show the minimal fix separately from suggested improvements. Let reviewers toggle between "show me only what changed" and "show me the reasoning." Or train on a synthetic dataset where the task is literally "make the smallest correct change," not "fix this bug."

The Cursor experience you mention is pretty common in my team too, but I've stopped seeing it as the model doing too much. It's more that the UI doesn't help you ask for what you actually want.

kids.text:
This conflates two separate things though. Yes, models are trained on refactoring patterns, but that's precisely the problem the post is identifying. When you're asking for a targeted fix in a code review context, you don't want the model applying "best practices" across the whole function-you want surgical precision.

The training data argument actually supports the author's point: if models learn to refactor because that's common in open source, they'll keep doing it even when it's the wrong behavior for this specific use case. That's not a reason to accept over-editing, it's a reason to train differently or add constraints that penalize unnecessary changes.

I actually tested this hypothesis by instrumenting a local Claude instance to track edit distances on ~200 bug fixes. The median edit distance was 3.2x larger than necessary. On simple off-by-one errors, it was closer to 5x.

What's interesting is that this mirrors a pattern I saw years ago with early autocomplete tools. They'd "helpfully" reformat your entire file while fixing one typo. The difference then was that we could just reject the change. Now with these models integrated directly into the editor, there's this psychological pressure to accept the whole diff because it *looks* professional and well-intentioned.

The real issue the parent raises though-training data bias toward refactoring-is spot on. Models see "touch code = refactor code" so often in GitHub that they've internalized it as law. But code review friction is real, and you can measure it. I'd rather have a dumb model that changes exactly three characters than a helpful one that rewrites the function, even correctly.

This is a great empirical finding, but I think the real lever here is regulatory. If we're moving toward mandating AI-assisted code review (which some enterprises are quietly doing), we need standards around edit distance and change scope. The FTC should be looking at this the same way they looked at dark patterns in UX. A model that systematically obscures intent through unnecessary refactoring is making security audits harder, which has downstream liability implications. The 5x edit distance on simple fixes isn't just annoying, it's a compliance nightmare waiting to happen.

I think you're diagnosing the symptom, not the disease. The real problem is that we've framed code assistance as a black box that should "understand intent" when really we need it to be a scalpel. We've optimized these models for throughput and general capability instead of precision on a specific, constrained task. If a model could *only* apply the minimal diff to fix the stated problem, it would be useless at 90% of what people ask it to do. So either we need much better prompting discipline, or we need a fundamentally different tool architecture. But asking GPT to "fix this one bug" and expecting it to respect code ownership is like asking a chef to fix your undersalted soup and being surprised when he rewrites your entire recipe.
